words.shortDefinition: Spending disproportionate time on trivial decisions while ignoring important ones.
words.longDefinition: The tendency to focus excessive attention on minor, easy-to-grasp details (like the color of a bikeshed) while avoiding complex, critical decisions (like nuclear reactor design). A productivity anti-pattern in engineering teams.
words.techContext: Invoked when teams spend too long debating minor issues like code formatting or naming conventions instead of addressing architecture or performance concerns.
words.exampleSentence: We spent two hours bikeshedding the button color instead of discussing the database migration strategy.
words.etymology: from Parkinson's Law of Triviality (1957) — hypothetical committee debates bikeshed color endlessly.

words.shortDefinition: A chain of seemingly unrelated tasks that must be done before accomplishing the original goal.
words.longDefinition: When you want to fix A, but first need to update B, which requires configuring C, which depends on installing D... and now you're yak shaving. Coined after a Ren & Stimpy episode. Often a sign of complex dependencies or scope creep.
words.techContext: Common when setting up development environments or untangling technical debt. Recognizing yak shaving helps teams question whether all steps are truly necessary.
words.exampleSentence: I just wanted to add a button, but three hours of yak shaving later I'm upgrading Node, fixing tests, and updating CI config.
words.etymology: coined by Carlin Vieri at MIT (2000) inspired by Ren & Stimpy's 'Yak Shaving Day' episode.

codeExample.code:
# Goal: deploy new feature
# But first:
npm update    # yak shave 1
fix tests     # yak shave 2
upgrade CI    # yak shave 3
# ... still haven't deployed the feature

words.shortDefinition: Explaining your code line-by-line to an inanimate object to find bugs.
words.longDefinition: From The Pragmatic Programmer: keep a rubber duck on your desk and explain problematic code to it out loud. The act of articulating the problem often reveals the solution. Works because teaching forces you to examine assumptions.
words.techContext: A surprisingly effective debugging technique. Many developers find bugs while writing a question for Stack Overflow before ever posting it.
words.exampleSentence: I spent an hour rubber duck debugging with my coworker's plant and found the off-by-one error immediately.
words.etymology: popularized by Hunt and Thomas in The Pragmatic Programmer (1999).

words.shortDefinition: Using your own product internally to identify bugs and usability issues before customers do.
words.longDefinition: Short for 'eating your own dog food.' Companies dogfood by using their own software in production. Microsoft, Google, and Apple famously dogfood pre-release builds. Catches real-world issues and builds empathy for users.
words.techContext: Internal dogfooding often catches bugs that QA misses. Also validates whether the product solves real problems.
words.exampleSentence: We started dogfooding the new API and immediately found three edge cases our test suite missed.
words.etymology: from 1970s Alpo dog food commercials where the president ate the product; popularized in tech by Microsoft in 1980s.

codeExample.code:
# Dogfooding example:
# Use your own CI/CD tool to deploy itself
# Use your own monitoring tool to monitor itself
# Ship internal teams to production first

words.shortDefinition: The number of team members who can be hit by a bus before the project is in serious trouble.
words.longDefinition: Measures knowledge concentration risk. A bus factor of 1 means only one person understands a critical system. Good teams aim for bus factor ≥3 via documentation, pairing, and cross-training.
words.techContext: Low bus factor signals fragility. When key engineers leave, teams scramble to understand their systems. Mitigate via knowledge sharing and docs.
words.exampleSentence: The auth system has a bus factor of one — only Sarah knows how it works, and she's on vacation.
words.etymology: morbid software engineering term from 1990s; sometimes euphemized as 'truck number' or 'lottery factor.'

codeExample.code:
# Bus factor assessment:
auth_service:
  owners: [sarah]
  bus_factor: 1  # ⚠️ risky

payment_service:
  owners: [alex, jordan, pat]
  bus_factor: 3  # ✅ safer

words.shortDefinition: Persistent self-doubt about one's abilities despite evidence of competence.
words.longDefinition: The feeling that you're a fraud who doesn't deserve your role and will soon be 'found out.' Extremely common in tech, especially among high-achievers. Recognizing it helps normalize the experience and reduces isolation.
words.techContext: Affects developers at all levels. Senior engineers often feel it more acutely because they're expected to know everything. Combat it by sharing struggles and celebrating learning.
words.exampleSentence: Even after shipping three major features, I had imposter syndrome during the promotion panel — I felt like I'd just gotten lucky.
words.etymology: coined by psychologists Clance and Imes in 1978; widely discussed in tech culture since 2010s.

codeExample.code:
# Common imposter syndrome thoughts:
# "Everyone else knows what they're doing"
# "I just Googled the solution — does that count?"
# "This feature worked but I don't know why"
# Reality: everyone feels this way sometimes.

words.shortDefinition: Two developers working together at one workstation, one typing while the other reviews in real-time.
words.longDefinition: Driver writes code while navigator reviews each line, suggests improvements, and thinks strategically. Roles switch frequently. Catches bugs early, shares knowledge, and produces higher-quality code. Core practice in Extreme Programming.
words.techContext: Effective for onboarding, complex problems, and knowledge transfer. Trade-off: doubles person-hours but reduces review cycles and debugging time.
words.exampleSentence: We pair programmed the payment integration — the navigator caught three potential race conditions before we even ran the code.
words.etymology: formalized in Extreme Programming (1996) by Kent Beck; practiced informally since earlier.

codeExample.code:
# Pair programming session:
# Driver: writes code, tactical focus
# Navigator: reviews, strategic thinking
# Switch roles every 20-30 minutes
# Result: fewer bugs, shared understanding

words.shortDefinition: Systematic examination of code changes by peers before merging to catch issues and share knowledge.
words.longDefinition: Reviewers check for bugs, design issues, readability, and consistency with standards. Provides feedback, catches errors, enforces best practices, and spreads knowledge across the team. Essential quality gate in modern development.
words.techContext: Good reviews are constructive, specific, and timely. Bad reviews nitpick style while missing logic errors. Many teams require 1-2 approvals before merge.
words.exampleSentence: The code review caught a SQL injection vulnerability I'd missed — the reviewer noticed we weren't sanitizing user input.
words.etymology: formal code review dates to 1970s IBM; GitHub pull requests (2008) democratized the practice.

codeExample.code:
## Code review checklist:
- [ ] Logic correct?
- [ ] Tests added?
- [ ] Security issues?
- [ ] Readable?
- [ ] Follows conventions?

words.shortDefinition: A blameless analysis of an incident to understand what went wrong and prevent recurrence.
words.longDefinition: After outages or major bugs, teams write postmortems documenting timeline, root cause, impact, and action items. Blameless means focusing on systems and processes, not individuals. SRE teams treat postmortems as learning opportunities.
words.techContext: Good postmortems are honest, detailed, and actionable. Bad postmortems finger-point or paper over systemic issues. Google's SRE book has excellent postmortem templates.
words.exampleSentence: The postmortem revealed the outage was caused by a deployment during peak traffic without staged rollout — we've since automated canary deploys.
words.etymology: from Latin 'post mortem' (after death); medical term adopted by software engineering for incident analysis.

codeExample.code:
## Postmortem Template:
**Incident:** Prod API down 45min
**Root cause:** DB connection pool exhausted
**Action items:**
- Add connection pool monitoring
- Document runbook
- Practice failover procedure

words.shortDefinition: Being responsible for responding to production incidents outside normal work hours.
words.longDefinition: Engineers rotate on-call shifts to handle alerts, outages, and emergencies 24/7. Good on-call practices: clear runbooks, fair rotation schedules, and blameless incident response. Sustainable on-call requires adequate tooling and staffing.
words.techContext: On-call burden correlates with service reliability. High-alert-frequency systems burn out engineers. Measure time to acknowledge, time to resolve, and false positive rate.
words.exampleSentence: I'm on-call this week — got paged at 2am for a database failover, but the runbook was clear so I resolved it in 10 minutes.
words.etymology: borrowed from medical practice where doctors are 'on-call' for emergencies; adopted by SRE teams.

codeExample.code:
# On-call rotation:
week_1: alex
week_2: jordan
week_3: pat
week_4: sarah

escalation:
  primary: 5 minutes
  secondary: 15 minutes
  manager: 30 minutes

words.shortDefinition: A fixed time period (usually 1-2 weeks) during which a team completes a set amount of work.
words.longDefinition: Fundamental unit of Scrum and agile development. Teams plan at sprint start, execute during sprint, demo at end, and retrospect afterward. Sprint length creates rhythm and predictability. Work not finished carries over to next sprint.
words.techContext: Sprints force regular delivery and reduce work-in-progress. Critics argue arbitrary time boxes can lead to rushed work or artificial deadlines.
words.exampleSentence: Our two-week sprints start Monday with planning, end Friday with demo and retro, then we immediately plan the next sprint.
words.etymology: term adopted from Scrum methodology (1990s); implies short, focused burst of effort.

codeExample.code:
## Sprint 42 (Jan 15-26):
- Plan auth redesign
- Implement OAuth flow
- Add MFA support
- Write migration runbook

Capacity: 80 story points
Actual: 72 points completed

words.shortDefinition: A brief daily meeting where team members share progress, plans, and blockers.
words.longDefinition: Also called daily scrum. Each person answers: what I did yesterday, what I'm doing today, and what's blocking me. Kept short (15 minutes) by literally standing. Synchronizes the team and surfaces blockers quickly.
words.techContext: Good standups are concise and action-oriented. Bad standups become status reports to managers. Remote teams often do async standups via Slack.
words.exampleSentence: In standup I mentioned the CI was broken, and Jordan immediately offered to pair on fixing it after the meeting.
words.etymology: from standing during the meeting to keep it brief; formalized in agile methodologies (1990s-2000s).

codeExample.code:
## Standup format:
**Alex:** Finished user auth, starting API integration. Blocked on API docs.
**Jordan:** Reviewed 3 PRs, deploying to staging today. No blockers.
**Pat:** Debugging payment issue. Need help from platform team.

words.shortDefinition: A meeting where the team reflects on what went well, what didn't, and how to improve.
words.longDefinition: Held at the end of each sprint. Team discusses wins, pain points, and experiments for next sprint. Requires psychological safety to be effective — blame kills honest retrospectives. Action items must be concrete and tracked.
words.techContext: Retros drive continuous improvement. Common formats: Start/Stop/Continue, Mad/Sad/Glad, or Timeline. Remote teams use tools like Retrium or Miro.
words.exampleSentence: In the retrospective we decided to stop scheduling meetings during focus hours and start using mob programming for complex features.
words.etymology: from Latin 'retrospectare' (to look back); formalized in agile as regular reflection practice.

codeExample.code:
## Sprint 42 Retro:
**What went well:** Shipped auth v2 ahead of schedule
**What didn't:** Too many meetings, lost focus time
**Action items:**
- [ ] Block 9-11am as no-meeting time
- [ ] Reduce standup to 10 minutes

words.shortDefinition: The amount of work a team completes per sprint, measured in story points or tasks.
words.longDefinition: Used for capacity planning and predicting delivery dates. Calculated as average completed story points over recent sprints. Velocity naturally varies — chasing higher velocity as a goal leads to inflated estimates and rushed work.
words.techContext: Velocity is a planning tool, not a performance metric. Comparing velocity across teams is meaningless — each team's points are calibrated differently.
words.exampleSentence: Our velocity averages 50 points per sprint, so we'll need four sprints to complete this 200-point epic.
words.etymology: borrowed from physics; applied to agile as 'speed of work delivery' in 1990s Scrum practices.

codeExample.code:
# Velocity tracking:
Sprint_40: 48 points
Sprint_41: 52 points
Sprint_42: 45 points
Average:   48 points

# Plan next sprint: 50 points (don't overcommit)

words.shortDefinition: Uncontrolled expansion of a project's requirements after work has begun.
words.longDefinition: Features or requirements added mid-project without adjusting timeline or resources. Scope creep kills deadlines and morale. Combat it with clear requirements, change management process, and saying no to 'just one more thing.'
words.techContext: Common symptom: 'while we're at it, could we also...' Agile mitigates scope creep via fixed sprints and prioritized backlogs.
words.exampleSentence: The payment feature suffered massive scope creep — what started as Stripe integration became a full fraud detection system.
words.etymology: project management term from 1990s; 'creep' implies slow, unnoticed expansion.

codeExample.code:
## Original scope:
- Integrate Stripe
- Handle successful payments

## Scope creep:
- Add PayPal, Apple Pay, Google Pay
- Implement fraud detection
- Build admin dashboard
- Export accounting reports
# ... now 6 weeks behind schedule

words.shortDefinition: A toggle that enables or disables functionality without deploying new code.
words.longDefinition: Feature flags (or feature toggles) allow shipping code to production in an off state, then enabling it via configuration. Enables gradual rollouts, A/B testing, and emergency kill switches. Essential for trunk-based development and continuous delivery.
words.techContext: Flags let you deploy incomplete features, test in production safely, and roll back instantly. Accumulating stale flags creates tech debt — clean them up after rollout.
words.exampleSentence: We deployed the new checkout behind a feature flag set to 10% of users, monitored errors for a day, then rolled it out to everyone.
words.etymology: emerged in continuous delivery practices (2000s-2010s); popularized by tools like LaunchDarkly.

words.shortDefinition: Minimum Viable Product - the smallest version that delivers value and enables learning.
words.longDefinition: An MVP has just enough features to satisfy early customers and provide feedback for future development. Not a prototype — it must actually work and provide value. The goal is to learn, not to launch a half-finished product.
words.techContext: Common mistake: confusing MVP with 'cheapest possible version.' A good MVP solves one problem well rather than many problems poorly.
words.exampleSentence: Our MVP was just Stripe payments and email receipts — we added invoicing, refunds, and reporting based on user feedback.
words.etymology: popularized by Eric Ries in The Lean Startup (2011); concept from agile and lean methodologies.

codeExample.code:
## E-commerce MVP:
✅ Product catalog
✅ Shopping cart
✅ Stripe checkout
✅ Email confirmation

❌ Wishlist (v2)
❌ Reviews (v2)
❌ Recommendations (v3)

words.shortDefinition: Proof of Concept - a small experiment to validate technical feasibility.
words.longDefinition: POCs test whether an approach will work before committing to full implementation. Meant to be throwaway code — don't ship POCs to production. Answers 'can we do this?' before investing in doing it properly.
words.techContext: Good POCs are time-boxed (1-3 days) and focus on the riskiest assumption. Bad POCs become production code because 'it already works.'
words.exampleSentence: We built a POC to see if Rust could handle our throughput requirements — it worked, so we rewrote the service properly.
words.etymology: project management term adopted by engineering; formalized in technology adoption processes.

codeExample.code:
# POC goal: Can WebAssembly improve performance?
# Time box: 2 days
# Success criteria: 2x speedup on benchmark
# Result: Yes → proceed with full implementation
# POC code: DELETE, start fresh with proper architecture

words.shortDefinition: An engineer who guides technical decisions and architecture while still writing code.
words.longDefinition: Tech leads balance hands-on coding with technical leadership. Responsibilities: architecture decisions, mentoring, code review, unblocking the team, and aligning technical work with product goals. Not a manager — still an individual contributor.
words.techContext: Good tech leads enable the team rather than becoming a bottleneck. They delegate, document decisions, and build consensus rather than dictating.
words.exampleSentence: As tech lead I wrote the initial service, reviewed all PRs, and ran weekly architecture discussions to align the team on patterns.
words.etymology: role emerged in 1990s-2000s as engineering teams scaled and needed technical leadership separate from management.

codeExample.code:
## Tech lead responsibilities:
- [ ] Define architecture
- [ ] Review critical PRs
- [ ] Mentor junior engineers
- [ ] Write design docs
- [ ] Unblock team members
- [ ] Still shipping code (30-50% time)

words.shortDefinition: Work In Progress - tasks started but not yet completed.
words.longDefinition: Kanban principle: limit WIP to improve flow and reduce context switching. Too much WIP means nothing gets finished. Pull requests labeled WIP (or draft) signal not ready for review. Low WIP correlates with higher throughput.
words.techContext: Many teams enforce WIP limits per column on Kanban boards. Finishing work is more valuable than starting new work.
words.exampleSentence: Our WIP limit is three tasks per engineer — when I finish one, I pull the next from the backlog instead of starting five things at once.
words.etymology: manufacturing term from lean production; adopted by agile software teams in 2000s.

codeExample.code:
## Kanban board:
Backlog | In Progress (WIP limit: 4) | Review | Done
   10   |    3 ✅                   |   2    |  15

# WIP limit enforced: can't start new work until In Progress < 4

words.shortDefinition: When git cannot automatically combine changes because two branches modified the same lines.
words.longDefinition: Occurs when merging or rebasing branches that edited overlapping code. Git marks conflicts with <<<<, ====, >>>> markers. Developer must manually resolve by choosing which changes to keep. Frequent conflicts suggest teams aren't communicating.
words.techContext: Minimize conflicts via small PRs, trunk-based development, and frequent pulls from main. Some conflicts are unavoidable and healthy.
words.exampleSentence: I had a merge conflict because both my branch and main had refactored the same auth function — I manually combined the changes.
words.etymology: term from version control systems; formalized with distributed VCS like git (2005).

codeExample.code:
<<<<<<< HEAD
function authenticate(user: User) {
  return jwt.sign(user.id);
=======
function authenticate(user: User) {
  return oauth.authorize(user);
>>>>>>> feature-branch

// Resolve manually by choosing or combining

words.shortDefinition: Pull Request - a proposal to merge code changes from one branch into another.
words.longDefinition: Developers create PRs to request code review before merging. PR includes diff, description, and discussion thread. Reviewers comment, request changes, or approve. GitHub popularized PRs; GitLab calls them merge requests.
words.techContext: Good PRs are focused, well-described, and appropriately sized (<400 lines). Bad PRs dump thousands of lines with no context.
words.exampleSentence: I opened a PR for the auth refactor, tagged two reviewers, and linked the Jira ticket in the description.
words.etymology: coined by GitHub (2008) for their git collaboration workflow; now ubiquitous in software development.

codeExample.code:
## PR #423: Add OAuth support

### Changes:
- Implemented OAuth flow
- Added login button
- Updated tests

### Testing:
- Manual: tested Google & GitHub login
- Automated: 15 new tests passing

Closes #401

words.shortDefinition: Rewriting commit history by moving commits to a new base, creating a linear history.
words.longDefinition: Rebasing replays your commits on top of another branch, avoiding merge commits. Creates clean, linear history but rewrites SHA hashes. Golden rule: never rebase commits pushed to shared branches. Use for local cleanup before pushing.
words.techContext: Interactive rebase lets you squash, reorder, or edit commits. Rebase vs merge is a religious debate — both have trade-offs.
words.exampleSentence: Before opening my PR, I rebased my feature branch onto main and squashed my 15 WIP commits into 3 logical commits.
words.etymology: git command introduced by Linus Torvalds (2005); 're-' + 'base' (new foundation for commits).

codeExample.code:
# Rebase feature branch onto main:
git checkout feature
git rebase main  # replays feature commits on latest main

# Interactive rebase (squash commits):
git rebase -i HEAD~5  # edit last 5 commits

words.shortDefinition: A single repository containing multiple projects or services, as opposed to one repo per project.
words.longDefinition: Monorepos centralize code for easier cross-project changes, atomic commits, and shared tooling. Google, Facebook, and Microsoft use monorepos at massive scale. Trade-offs: simpler dependency management vs. larger checkout size and complex build systems.
words.techContext: Monorepos require good tooling (Bazel, Nx, Turborepo) to remain fast. Not the same as a monolith — you can have microservices in a monorepo.
words.exampleSentence: We moved to a monorepo so updating a shared library didn't require coordinating releases across 10 separate repositories.
words.etymology: portmanteau of 'monolithic' + 'repository'; term emerged in 2010s as companies scaled version control strategies.

codeExample.code:
# Monorepo structure:
my-monorepo/
├── services/
│   ├── api/
│   ├── web/
│   └── worker/
├── packages/
│   ├── shared-ui/
│   └── utils/
└── package.json  # one lockfile, shared deps

words.shortDefinition: Shortcuts in code that save time now but slow down future development.
words.longDefinition: Ward Cunningham's metaphor: like financial debt, technical debt accrues interest (slower velocity) until repaid through refactoring. Some debt is deliberate and strategic; some is reckless and accidental. Successful teams allocate time to repay debt regularly.
words.techContext: Product managers often undervalue tech debt work because it's invisible to users. Frame debt paydown in terms of velocity: 'this will make us 2x faster at shipping features.'
words.exampleSentence: We accumulated tech debt by copy-pasting the validation logic instead of abstracting it — now fixing a bug requires updating 12 files.
words.etymology: coined by Ward Cunningham (1992); popularized by Martin Fowler who classified debt into quadrants (deliberate/accidental, reckless/prudent).
